metrics.name: Hormuz
metrics.question: Will daily oil tanker transits through the Strait of Hormuz reach 50 or more on any day between April 29 and June 1, 2026?

skipped_reasons: no deterministic trigger fired: primary stable, no verified evidence conflict, no reversal-capable disputed assumption (imagination alone is not a trigger)

simulation_result.question: Will daily oil tanker transits through the Strait of Hormuz reach 50 or more on any day between April 29 and June 1, 2026?
limitations: forecast decomposition — grounded prior 0.5 (counted n=2); simulation-conditional None (resolved mass 0.00); headline 0.5 via prior_only_simulation_unavailable. Prior and simulation are reported separately and never blended by a fixed rule.
unresolved [unresolved_missing_mechanism]: 1.000 — one repair attempt else under-modeled disclosure

no leakage-audited prior↔simulation reliability combiner is fitted — the headline is the simulation-conditional forecast; the grounded prior is reported separately; this is a stated reason Lean V2 must not become the default yet

blueprint.causal_thesis: The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed due to Iranian mining and a US naval blockade, with only ~7 ships/day and no oil tankers as of April 27. The EIA assumes the conflict does not persist, but no diplomatic or military breakthrough is evident by April 30. Without a coordinated de-mining a

dropped_grounded_rates.why: basis_quote not found verbatim in evidence

pruned.reason: ceremonial: no discretion over any surviving terminal pathway

hormuz_closure_regime.claim: The Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to commercial oil tanker traffic due to military conflict and blockade.

provenance.definition: The Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to commercial oil tanker traffic due to military conflict and blockade.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Iran shut the Strait of Hormuz and mined the waterway after U.S.-Israeli strikes killed Supreme Leader Khamenei on February 28, 2026.
cases.basis_quote: Iran shut the strait and reportedly mined parts of the waterway; the U.S. subsequently imposed a naval blockade of Iranian ports.

cases.description: As of April 27, 2026, only about 7 ships transited the strait in the prior 24 hours, none of which were oil tankers carrying cargo for the global market.
cases.basis_quote: As of April 27, 2026, only about 7 ships transited the strait in the prior 24 hours, none of which were oil tankers carrying cargo for the global market.

pre_conflict_traffic_baseline.claim: Pre-conflict daily oil tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz was approximately 125-140 ships per day, with oil tankers comprising a significant portion.

provenance.definition: Pre-conflict daily oil tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz was approximately 125-140 ships per day, with oil tankers comprising a significant portion.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Pre-conflict, between 125 and 140 ships typically crossed in and out of the strait daily.
cases.basis_quote: Pre-conflict, between 125 and 140 ships typically crossed in and out of the strait daily.

us_blockade_policy.claim: The U.S. imposed a naval blockade of Iranian ports after the strait closure, preventing Iranian oil tankers from transiting.

provenance.definition: The U.S. imposed a naval blockade of Iranian ports after the strait closure, preventing Iranian oil tankers from transiting.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Iran oil tankers turned back by US blockade, Hormuz traffic sparse.
cases.basis_quote: Iran oil tankers turned back by US blockade, Hormuz traffic sparse.

outcome_reference_class.quantity: daily oil tanker transits through Strait of Hormuz reach 50 or more

provenance.definition: On any calendar day (UTC) between April 29, 2026 and June 1, 2026 inclusive, at least 50 commercial oil tankers transit the Strait of Hormuz.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: As reported by Reuters, Lloyd's List, or Bloomberg.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Days with fewer than 50 tanker transits; reports from other sources not listed.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Pre-conflict, daily oil tanker traffic was approximately 62-70 tankers per day, well above 50.
cases.basis_quote: Pre-conflict total vessel traffic was 125–140 ships/day, and normal commercial oil tanker traffic is at least 50 tanker transits per day.

cases.description: On April 27, 2026, only about 7 ships transited, none oil tankers, so far below 50.
cases.basis_quote: As of April 27, 2026, only about 7 ships transited the strait in the prior 24 hours, none of which were oil tankers carrying cargo for the global market.

reuters_lloyds_bloomberg_reporting.quorum: At least one of the three sources must report the milestone.
reuters_lloyds_bloomberg_reporting.consequence_of_nonparticipation: If none report by deadline, question resolves NO.

weight_invariant.rule: every weight traces to a counted reference class or is explicit unknown mass; no qualitative label is mapped to a number

iran_military.claim: Iran maintains full closure and mining of the Strait to maximize strategic leverage and deter any U.S. or coalition attempt to reopen it, accepting near-zero commercial traffic.
distinguishing_observations: Continued mine sightings
No IRGC-N patrols allowing transits
Official statements threatening any transit
iran_military.action_if_state: Iranian military actively enforces closure by firing on or seizing any tanker attempting transit, maintains minefields, and denies safe passage to all commercial vessels.

iran_military.claim: Iran allows a trickle of non-Western, sanctioned-state tankers (e.g., Chinese, Russian) to transit under escort to demonstrate partial control and split the international coalition, while still blocking most traffic.
distinguishing_observations: Occasional unflagged or Chinese-flagged tanker transits
IRGC-N escort of specific vessels
No attacks on those vessels
iran_military.action_if_state: Iranian military selectively permits a few tankers (fewer than 10 per day) to pass, while maintaining mines and threatening all others, aiming to create diplomatic fractures.

us_navy.claim: U.S. Navy enforces a comprehensive naval blockade of all Iranian ports and interdicts any tanker attempting to enter or exit the Strait, regardless of flag, to maximize economic pressure on Iran.
historical_case_refs: U.S. interdiction in Persian Gulf 1987-1988 (Operation Earnest Will)
distinguishing_observations: All tankers attempting transit are boarded or turned back
No exceptions for humanitarian or neutral flags
U.S. announces expanded blockade zone
us_navy.action_if_state: U.S. Navy stops, boards, and diverts every tanker approaching the Strait, using warning shots and disabling fire if necessary, maintaining near-zero transit numbers.

us_navy.claim: U.S. Navy focuses blockade on Iranian ports but does not systematically stop all Strait transits, allowing some non-Iranian tankers to pass if they are not carrying Iranian oil or sanctioned cargo.
historical_case_refs: U.S. sanctions enforcement in Gulf 2019-2020 (targeting Iranian oil only)
distinguishing_observations: Some tankers (e.g., Iraqi, Kuwaiti) allowed through after inspection
U.S. statements emphasize 'Iranian oil' not all traffic
No boarding of clearly non-Iranian vessels
us_navy.action_if_state: U.S. Navy intercepts only vessels suspected of carrying Iranian oil or heading to Iranian ports, allowing other tankers to transit after cursory checks, potentially enabling 50+ transits if non-Irania

tanker_operators.claim: Tanker operators assess the Strait as too dangerous due to mines, military confrontation, and insurance void, and refuse to transit regardless of potential profit or diplomatic assurances.
historical_case_refs: Tanker operators' response to 2019 Hormuz tanker attacks
distinguishing_observations: No commercial tanker transits for weeks
War risk insurance premiums at 10%+ of hull value
Major shipping lines publicly refuse to sail
tanker_operators.action_if_state: Tanker operators keep all vessels away from the Strait, rerouting around Africa or waiting outside, resulting in zero or near-zero daily transits.

tanker_operators.claim: Tanker operators are willing to transit if provided credible military escort (e.g., U.S. or coalition convoys) and if insurance is reinstated, but only in limited numbers and under strict conditions.
distinguishing_observations: Tankers only transit in convoy with naval escort
Transit numbers remain below 20 per day
Operators demand government compensation for risk
tanker_operators.action_if_state: Tanker operators send a small number of vessels (fewer than 10 per day) through the Strait under coalition escort, but refuse to increase volume until mines are cleared and threat eliminated.

tanker_operators.claim: Tanker operators believe the Strait is safe enough for routine commercial transit, due to mine clearance, cessation of hostilities, or credible security guarantees, and resume normal scheduling.
distinguishing_observations: Multiple tankers transit daily without military escort
Insurance premiums drop to near pre-conflict levels
Major oil companies resume regular shipments
tanker_operators.action_if_state: Tanker operators resume normal transit schedules, with 50+ tankers per day passing through the Strait, as they did before the conflict.

iran_clear_mines.description: Iranian military removes mines from strait

us_lift_blockade.description: US Navy allows non-Iranian tankers to pass

tanker_transit.description: Tanker operators send vessels through strait
tanker_transit.validation: Mines cleared AND blockade lifted AND operators decide to transit

novel_cast_vote_blockade.description: The us_navy records a vote to uphold the blockade as a pressure tactic until Iran demonstrates concrete concessions, maintaining strategic position.
